text:
This idea used to be called RAD - Rapid Application Development.  Today, it is espoused in the Religion of Agile development.  It used to be a thrust in some pre-CL Lisps.

The idea is that you HAVE to screw around with ideas to try to figure out what the requirements are.  Users will NEVER specify enough details, so, it is incumbent upon you - the Architect and the Engineer - to figure out what the requirements are.

Screwing around with ideas needs to be supported by languages that make throwing prototypes away easy.  Building elaborate type systems is counter-productive when screwing around with ideas.  Sunk Cost Fallacy - if you’ve spent lots of time working out the details of a type system, you are less likely to want to throw it away.  Instead you will want to tweak the elaborate type system, even when that doesn’t make sense.

Just about all of our popular languages cause you to enter into the domain of Sunk Cost Fallacy.  Predict the Future.  Over-confidence in the correct-itude of a design.  No room for screwing around and throwing code away.

After several, iterative rounds of screwing around, you might Production-Engineer an idea and turn it into an actual product.  This is the point where current popular languages (Haskell, Python, Rust, etc.) become useful.

root.text: Had an interesting experience this week, I'd like to share about the effect of changing the visual context of my tool...:thread:

text: The idea that visual cues set the context for perception and thinking has been around for a long time in various settings, but it seems that it doesn't have a name. Outside of tech, there's "broken windows", but also "dress for the job the want, not the one you have". Mental visualization as a technique for achieving goals plays a big role in Neuro-Linguistic Programming. And some ideas of Feng Shui go in the same direction, though I am careful here because all I know about Feng Shui comes from a single conversation with an expert practitioner.

text:
Yes, riffing on Konrad’s note, I would add phrases such as “language affects thought”, “a picture is worth a thousand words”, etc.

I conjecture that purveyors of statically-typed, textual languages “see” structure “in their heads”, but feel forced to pound the structure down for use with pointy sticks and clay tablets.

In Physics, I learned that to understand a seemingly-complicated problem required breaking the problem down (“divide and conquer”) then creating a unique notation for that problem aspect (a different notation for each aspect) and to apply “simplifying assumptions” to the new notation to make the aspect-under-scrutiny seem less complicated.  While, of course, remaining cognizant of the simplifying assumptions and remaining cognizant of not using the notation beyond its sweet spot.

I think that software development is like that, too.  You drill down on a particular aspect of a problem, ignoring the rest.  When you come up for air, you see other aspects that need to be solved.  All popular programming languages are the same and force you to think inside the same kind of box.  Simpler solutions to a problem might not be apparent, nor seem very simple from inside the single box.

Maybe what is being experienced is the freedom to think - to solve a problem instead of force-fitting the problem for expression within the confines of a single given notation or a single style of thought.

text: I suspect that programming languages and systems still suffer from early computing technology. In particular batch mode, with long feedback cycles. Think before you code. Run code in your head before punching it into expensive cards. Those days are gone!

elements.text: I suspect that programming languages and systems still suffer from early computing technology. In particular batch mode, with long feedback cycles. Think before you code. Run code in your head before punching it into expensive cards. Those days are gone!

text:
<https://scrapscript.org/>

Scrapscript is best understood through a few perspectives:
• “it’s JSON with types and functions and hashed references”
• “it’s tiny Haskell with extreme syntactic consistency”
• “it’s a language with a weird IPFS thing”  
• <https://scrapscript.org/#ContentAddressibleEverything|Content-Addressible Everything>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#WorldwideCollaborativeNamespace|Worldwide Collaborative Namespace>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#NoBrokenDependencies|No Broken Dependencies>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#ExpressionLevelVersioning|Expression-Level Versioning>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#TimeTravelInterpreter|Time-Travel Interpreter>
• <https://scrapscript.org/#ThePlatformParadigm|The “Platform” Paradigm>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#DesignedForEmbeddedDSLs|Designed For Embedded DSLs>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#SelfDocumentingTypedConfigs|Self-Documenting Typed Configs>
• <https://scrapscript.org/#LargeAsALanguageSmallAsAMessage|Large As A Language; Small As A Message>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#AllValidProgramsReturnValidPrograms|All Valid Programs Return Valid Programs>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#SendArbitraryTypesOverTheWire|Send Arbitrary Types Over The Wire>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#SendUnevaluatedSandboxedPrograms|Send Unevaluated Sandboxed Programs>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#ComesWithFlatBinaryRepresentation|Comes With “Flat” Binary Representation>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#MagicCompression|Magic Compression>
• <https://scrapscript.org/#FirstClassNetworkRequests|First-Class Network Requests>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#SerializationFreeExperience|Serialization-Free Experience>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#TypecheckAcrossNetworkBounds|Typecheck Across Network Bounds>
• <https://scrapscript.org/#ToolingFromFirstPrinciples|Tooling From First-Principles>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#OptimizedForAIAutocomplete|Optimized For AI & Autocomplete>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#SnippetsOnSteroids|Snippets On Steroids>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#SeamlesslyPublishAndPartake|Seamlessly Publish And Partake>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#HostingAccountsAndPayments|Hosting, Accounts, And Payments>
    ◦ <https://scrapscript.org/#BrandNewBrowser|Brand New Browser>
